overview.name: OwnStack CLI
overview.tagline: Heroku-style ergonomics on infrastructure you own.
overview.description:
OwnStack is a control plane for deploying apps onto stacks you own.
The CLI scaffolds composable apps, drives local development, and talks
to the control-plane API for deploys, logs, SSH, and more.

Two big ideas:
  - Apps are composed from atomic components (rails, nestjs, react, angular, docs, wordpress).
  - Stacks are infrastructure you own (AWS, GCP, OpenStack, bare metal). One control plane drives many stacks.

Configuration:
  - Global:  ~/.ownstack/config   (OWNSTACK_API_URL, OWNSTACK_API_TOKEN)
  - Project: .ownstack-config     (OWNSTACK_APP_ID or APP_NAME, OWNSTACK_APP_TOKEN)
  - Linked: .ownstack-app         (app id; created by `ownstack remote -a`)
overview.install:
macOS / Linux:
  curl -fsSL https://ownstack.org/install.sh | bash

From a local clone:
  cd /path/to/ownstack/cli && ./install.sh

Verify:
  ownstack --help
overview.auth:
Most control-plane and deploy commands need auth.
  ownstack login                       # browser device-code flow
  ownstack login --token YOUR_TOKEN    # direct (from Settings → API token)
Tokens live in ~/.ownstack/config. Override the API URL with OWNSTACK_API_URL.

groups.title: App generation
groups.body: Scaffold apps locally from atomic components. No control plane required.

commands.name: generate
commands.summary: Generate a composable app from atomic components.

flags.desc: Push to ownstack Git after generating (requires login).
Skip interactive prompts (fully non-interactive).
Numeric suffix when multiple apps share one base.

examples.desc: Interactive — prompts for name and components.
Named app, interactive component picker.

examples.cmd: ownstack generate myapp rails react docs
examples.desc: Full stack — Rails API + React frontend + docs site.
Backend only.
NestJS + Angular.
Fully non-interactive.
Generate and push to ownstack Git.

commands.notes: Available components: rails, nestjs, react, angular, docs, wordpress.

commands.name: regenerate
commands.summary: Regenerate an existing app from its saved config.

examples.desc: Regenerate by app name.
Run from inside an app dir to refresh templates.
Regenerate only the Rails component.
Regenerate only SSL certificates.

commands.name: list
commands.summary: List available components.

commands.name: info
commands.summary: Show details about a component.

groups.title: Local development
groups.body: Run apps locally with Docker Compose or directly. Most commands run from inside the app directory.

commands.name: local start
commands.summary: Start, stop, restart, or tear down local services.

flags.desc: Detached mode.
Rebuild images before starting.

examples.desc: Start backend in foreground.
Detached.
Rebuild then start.
Start a specific service.
Stop and remove containers.

commands.name: local open
commands.summary: Open the local app URL in a browser.

commands.name: local run
commands.summary: Run a command inside a local service container.

commands.name: local stack
commands.summary: Manage a local Dokku stack (Docker container) for dev/testing.

examples.desc: Show status.
Stop and remove.

commands.name: connect
commands.summary: Open the control plane web UI to link a local app.

flags.desc: Use the local control plane URL.

groups.title: Control-plane commands
groups.body: Talk to the control-plane API. Most require `ownstack login` first.

commands.name: remote
commands.summary: Show or set the control-plane app linked to this repo.

flags.desc: Link to the given app (by name or id).
Replace an existing link in this directory (overwrites .ownstack-app / .ownstack-config).

examples.desc: Show the currently linked app.
Link this repo to an app.
Re-link a directory that already points to a different app.

commands.notes:
Creates `.ownstack-app` and stores the deploy token in `.ownstack-config`.
If the directory is already linked to a *different* app, linking is refused so you
don't clobber another app's link by standing in the wrong repo (a common footgun with
`ownstack app create`, which auto-links the current directory). Pass `--relink` to
override, or `cd` to the intended app's directory first. Re-linking to the same app is
always allowed (it just refreshes the deploy token).

commands.name: remote logs
commands.summary: Fetch app logs via the control plane (no SSH needed).

flags.desc: Last N lines (default 100).

examples.desc: Stream.

commands.name: remote deploy-log
commands.summary: View deployment (build + release) logs — same as the UI's "View log".

flags.desc: Target app explicitly.
Specific deployment id.

commands.name: remote agent
commands.summary: Claude Code on the stack — status, sessions, headless runs, interactive console.

flags.desc: Target app (or link via `ownstack remote -a`).
JSON output for status/sessions/show.
Stream output until the run completes.
Specific session.
Inline rollout message.

examples.desc: Stdin prompt, then stream.
Interactive Claude; full repo on stack host (needs jq).

commands.notes:
Requires jq for `run`, `rollout`, `console`, and human-readable `status` / `sessions` / `show`.
`console` calls GET …/claude_code/bootstrap on each open; cap defaults to 90/hour per app,
tunable with CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_BOOTSTRAP_PER_HOUR. Over the cap returns 429 (claude_bootstrap_rate_limited).
The "dangerously skip permissions" mode defaults on; respect features.claude_code_dangerously_skip_permissions.

groups.title: Signup invitations
groups.body:
Superuser commands for signup-access invitations, mirroring the
/admin/signup-access web UI. An invitation is a one-time access key
that lets someone sign up (for a new account) or join an existing
account. Give it an email and the invitee is emailed the signup link;
omit the email to get a raw token you share yourself.

commands.name: invites list
commands.summary: List invitations, most recent first.

flags.desc: Emit raw API JSON instead of the formatted table.

examples.desc: Show id, kind, status, account, email lock, and created date.

commands.name: invites create
commands.summary: Create an invitation and print the one-time access key.

flags.desc: Existing account to join (implies --kind join_account).
Restrict redemption to this address and email them the signup link.
Internal note shown in the list.
Override the kind (default join_account if --account is given, else new_account).
Emit raw API JSON instead of formatted output.

examples.desc: New-account invite, emailed to the invitee.
Invite someone to join the acme account.
Raw new-account token you share out of band (no email).

commands.name: invites revoke
commands.summary: Revoke a pending invitation.

examples.desc: Revoke invitation id 42.

groups.notes:
Requires a superuser token (the backend returns 403 otherwise) and jq for
formatted output and account-name/slug resolution. The access key is shown
only once at creation; it is stored hashed and cannot be retrieved later.

groups.title: Deployment
groups.body: Push code through the control plane. The control plane clones your repo and pushes to Dokku on the stack.

commands.name: deploy
commands.summary: Trigger a git-based deploy via the control plane.

flags.desc: Stream deployment output until complete. If a deploy is already in progress, attaches to it instead of erroring.
Target a specific stack (for multi-stack apps).
Deploy from a local Docker image instead of git.
Clear stuck deployments before deploying.

examples.desc: Deploy the linked app.
Deploy by name.

commands.notes:
`--wait` attaches to an in-progress deploy rather than failing with "a deploy is
already in progress", so re-running `deploy --wait` always ends up streaming the live
deployment. Use `deploy:status` for a one-shot, non-interactive status read.

commands.name: deploy:status
commands.summary: Print the latest deployment status per stack (non-interactive; for agents/CI).

flags.desc: Only show the given stack.
Emit the latest-per-stack deployments as JSON.

examples.desc: Latest status per stack for the linked app.

commands.notes:
Reads /api/apps/:id/deployments so you do not have to scrape `ownstack app info`.
Exit code reflects the most recent deployment: 0 deployed/idle, 1 failed, 2 in-progress.

commands.name: run
commands.summary: Run a one-off command in the app's container via the control plane (like heroku run).

flags.desc: Target app (otherwise the first argument, or the linked repo).
Run on a specific stack (for multi-stack apps).
Use the buffered endpoint instead of streaming (returns all output at the end).

examples.desc: Run in the linked app.

examples.cmd: ownstack run myapp -- rails runner 'puts User.count'

commands.notes:
The control plane SSHes to the stack with its stored key, so no local SSH access is
needed (unlike `ownstack remote run`, which shells out from your machine). Output streams
live by default so long tasks (e.g. a data backfill) do not hit the proxy timeout; the
command's remote exit code becomes the exit code of `ownstack run`. Use `--` before the
command so its own flags are not parsed by ownstack. App resolution: `--app`, else the
linked repo, else the first positional argument.

commands.name: git push
commands.summary: Push current directory to OwnStack Git (Gitea). Creates repo, optionally links to an app.

flags.desc: New repo name.
Link the new repo to an existing app.

examples.desc: From a linked app dir.

groups.title: Stack management
groups.body: Operations on stack infrastructure — plugins, data, backups, patches, maintenance.

commands.name: stack create
commands.summary: Provision a new managed stack (AWS, GCP, Azure, OpenStack, DigitalOcean, or SSH-managed). Mirrors the dashboard "New Stack" form.

flags.desc: Cloud provider (required).
Stored stack credential (required for managed providers). Resolves by name via /api/stack_credentials.
Cloud region (required for aws/azure/openstack/digitalocean; e.g. DigitalOcean nyc3, sfo3).
GCP project to deploy into.
Desired RAM in GiB (2..128).
Root disk in GB (10..1000).
For provider=ssh; remote SSH host.
For provider=ssh; SSH port (default 22).
For provider=ssh; SSH username.
For provider=ssh; path to a private-key file.
For provider=ssh; password (use instead of --ssh-private-key).

examples.desc: GCP n2-highmem-8 sized stack (8 vCPU, 64 GiB).
AWS r6i.2xlarge sized stack.
DigitalOcean droplet (s-1vcpu-2gb) for OSS-app dogfooding.
Adopt an existing host you SSH into.

commands.name: stack destroy
commands.summary: Tear down a stack and deprovision its cloud resources. Symmetric with `stack create`. Interactive confirmation by default; use `--yes` for scripts.

flags.desc: Skip the interactive confirmation prompt (required for non-interactive callers).

examples.desc: Interactive — prompts you to retype the stack name.
Non-interactive, by id.

commands.name: stack unmanaged
commands.summary: List orphan cloud instances — instances that exist under the account's AWS/GCP credentials but are tracked by no managed stack (e.g. left behind by a deleted stack or a crashed provision, still billing). CLI parity with the web Stacks page and `GET /api/stacks/unmanaged`. GCP credentials with no associated stack have no derivable project and are reported as a scan notice rather than scanned.

flags.desc: Emit the raw API payload (count, instances, errors, scanned, timed_out) instead of the table.

examples.desc: List orphan instances for the current profile's account.
Raw JSON for a specific account, e.g. for scripting.

commands.name: stack unmanaged destroy
commands.summary: Terminate an orphan instance by its instance id. Looks the instance up in the live scan to find the provider-specific parameters, then calls `DELETE /api/stacks/unmanaged`, which refuses anything that backs a managed stack. Not reversible. Interactive confirmation by default.

flags.desc: Skip the confirmation prompt (required for non-interactive callers).

examples.desc: Interactive — prompts before terminating the cloud instance.
Non-interactive teardown of a known orphan.

commands.name: stack containers
commands.summary: List docker containers on a stack with state classification (running, exited, unhealthy, orphan, stale). Stale containers are running but not in their app's current ps:scale — the usual cause of "missing" memory after repeated ps:rebuild or OOM-restart cycles.

flags.desc: Only list containers belonging to this Dokku app.
Filter to one state. Stale = running but outside ps:scale.
Emit raw JSON instead of the table view.

examples.desc: All containers across all apps on the stack.
Just the stale Sidekiq survivors for one app.

commands.name: stack reconcile-ps
commands.summary: Reap containers not in `dokku ps:scale`. Use this to clean up OOM-restart survivors that Dokku stopped tracking. Default is live reap; use --dry-run to preview.

flags.desc: Target stack (otherwise uses the linked app's stack).
Identify what would be removed without invoking `docker rm`.

examples.desc: Preview the reap.
Actually reap.

commands.name: stack resize-disk
commands.summary: Grow the stack's root volume. AWS extends the volume online (instance stays up); OpenStack stops the server, extends, then restarts.
commands.notes:
New size must be greater than the current size and between 10 and 1000 GB.
The filesystem is grown automatically after the volume extends. Watch
progress with `ownstack stack disk-usage <STACK>`.

examples.desc: Grow staging-hub's root volume to 500 GB (AWS, online).

commands.name: stack resize-ram
commands.summary: Change the instance RAM. Stops and restarts the instance to apply the new machine type.
commands.notes:
Supported sizes (GB): 2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128. The instance is stopped,
resized, and restarted, so expect brief downtime.

examples.desc: Resize to 16 GB RAM (causes a restart).

commands.name: stack add-local
commands.summary: Create a local stack (dev only).

commands.name: stack plugin-install
commands.summary: Install a Dokku plugin on a stack.

flags.desc: Target stack (otherwise prompts/uses default).

commands.name: stack data
commands.summary: List databases and manage volumes on a stack.

commands.name: stack backup
commands.summary: Run, list, enable, and inspect stack backups.

flags.desc: For `enable`. application = per-database dumps (restorable via `app data restore`); native = provider disk snapshot + AMI (whole-server restore); both = one nightly job produces both. Default native.
For `enable`. Schedule, default 0 3 * * *.
For `enable`. Completed backups to keep, default 7.

examples.desc: Lists job id, status, type, and timestamp. Use the job id with `app data restore --backup=ID`.
Nightly disk snapshot AND database dumps in one job.

commands.name: app data restore
commands.summary: Restore an app's database from a stack backup job (dokku postgres:import / mysql:import).

flags.desc: Stack backup job id (required, from `stack backup list`).
Stack the backup lives on. Defaults to the app's stack.

Service dump inside the backup to import. Enables cross-app restores, e.g. restore postgres-myapp into postgres-myapp-staging. Without it, only an exact match on the app's own linked service is accepted; on mismatch the error lists available services.

Where to read the dump from. Default prefers the on-host dump and falls back to S3 if the host file is gone. Pass s3 to restore from the object-storage copy (works cross-stack, e.g. restore prod's S3 dump onto staging); host to force the on-host file.

examples.desc: Restore the app's own dump from backup job 42.
Seed staging from the production dump in the same backup.
Stream the production dump straight from S3 into staging (no host copy needed).

commands.name: stack patches
commands.summary: List or apply stack patches (severity, deadlines, auto-on-deploy).
commands.body:
Patches are small fixes / installs the control plane manages per stack
(Dokku tweaks, ownstackd install, etc.). Mandatory patches with a
required_by date and auto_apply_on_deploy: true (currently: ownstackd)
run automatically before each deploy to the stack. Manual apply is
still available for backfilling dormant stacks.

The listing shows severity (mandatory/recommended/optional), an
[OVERDUE] tag past required_by, and an [auto on deploy] tag for
patches that will self-apply on the next deploy.

examples.desc: Show patches with status, severity, overdue, and auto-on-deploy flags.
Apply all pending patches now (or wait for the next deploy if they're auto-apply).
Fleet view, stacks with overdue mandatory patches.
Same, JSON output (pipe to jq for scripting).

commands.name: stack disk-usage
commands.summary: Show disk usage on a stack (via SSH).

commands.name: stack info
commands.summary: Host/container facts read live over SSH (cores, RAM, container memory limit, disk type).

flags.desc: Emit the facts as JSON.

commands.notes:
Surfaces CPU cores, total host RAM, the container memory limit (cgroup; "unlimited" means
bounded by host RAM), and whether the root disk is SSD or rotational, plus the provisioned
sizes. Makes correct DB/app tuning a read instead of an SSH expedition (free -h,
/sys/fs/cgroup/memory.max, lsblk -d -o NAME,ROTA by hand).

commands.name: stack memory
commands.summary: Host memory + per-container breakdown from the on-box heartbeat agent.
commands.body:
Reads the latest stack_health_snapshot and prints host RAM usage plus a
per-container table sorted by memory consumption. UI parity with the
Memory tab on the stack detail page.

Requires the heartbeat agent to be installed on the stack (it pushes
per-container Docker memory every 60s). If "No memory metrics yet" is
shown, run `ownstack add-on heartbeat <STACK>` to install.

flags.desc: Emit the latest_snapshot + recent_memory shape as JSON.

commands.name: stack reboot
commands.summary: Graceful reboot of the stack's host VM via the provider SDK.
commands.body:
Triggers a host-level reboot through the provider (EC2 reboot_instances,
GCP instances.reset, OpenStack server.reboot). Returns immediately
with a StackHostAction id; poll progress with `stack host-actions`.

Use when sshd has wedged but the hypervisor still reports the
instance as running (the classic "ssh banner timeout while AWS says
ok ok" failure mode). Volumes persist across the reboot.

commands.name: stack start
commands.summary: Power on a stopped host.

commands.name: stack stop
commands.summary: Power off the host (volumes persist).
commands.body:
Stops the underlying VM. Same effect as the cloud console's "stop"
button. Storage is preserved; the instance keeps its EBS volume(s)
and can be restarted with `stack start`.

commands.name: stack host-actions
commands.summary: Recent host start/stop/reboot history (last 25 entries).

commands.name: stack ownstackd
commands.summary: Manage the on-stack ownstackd daemon (replaces SSH-from-control-plane for routine ops).
commands.body:
ownstackd is a small Rust daemon that runs on a stack and executes
jobs sent from the control plane. Opt-in per stack: when enabled,
paths like `ownstack app status` skip the inline SSH and route work
through the daemon instead. When disabled, the stack keeps working
exactly as before, the daemon idles, and an SSH login MOTD tells the
operator how to reconnect or fully uninstall.

Source (private): github.com/ownstack-org/ownstackd

examples.desc: Show enabled / alive / last-seen / version.
Generate a stack-scoped token, flip the flag, print install one-liner.
Flip the flag off. Token kept so re-enable is one command. Daemon idles.
Invalidate the existing token and issue a new one. Reinstall daemon with the new token.

commands.name: stack maintenance
commands.summary: Prune images, schedule jobs, forecast disk, scan CVEs.

examples.desc: Days until 80% / 90% full.
Trivy scan of dokku/* images.

commands.name: stack share
commands.summary: Share a stack with another OwnStack account by email.
commands.body:
Shared stacks are full n:n access: the invited account deploys apps,
manages services, and sees everything on the stack, exactly like the
owner. The owner keeps two things: deleting the stack and managing
who it's shared with. No per-seat gating, no artificial restrictions.
It's your hardware; share it freely.

The invitee gets an email link (clicking it accepts), and the stack
also appears dimmed in their dashboard stack list with accept/reject
buttons. Any user of the invited account can accept on its behalf.
If the email has no OwnStack account yet, the invitation binds when
they sign up with that address.

examples.desc: Invite an account by email. Shows as pending until accepted.
List shares with status (pending / accepted) and who accepted.
Revoke a share. The account immediately loses access.
Accept a stack shared with your account (recipient side).
Reject a pending invitation (recipient side).
Leave a stack that was shared with you. Apps your account deployed keep running.

groups.title: Clusters and load balancing
groups.body:
A cluster pools stacks behind a hostname. It owns a Caddy proxy app (deployed to N proxy stacks
for redundancy) and routes traffic to member app-stacks. Pick the edge mode at create time:
  - dns_rr      DNS round-robin. Free, works anywhere.
  - cloudflare  Proxied origins, sub-second failover.
  - aws_alb     AWS ALB (LBaaS).

The proxy app is just an OwnStack app — same logs / restart / SSH / scale / maintenance as
anything else. The cluster regenerates its Caddyfile on member changes and dokku-deploys it.

commands.name: cluster list
commands.summary: List clusters in the active profile.

commands.name: cluster show
commands.summary: Show edge / proxies / members / status for a cluster.

commands.name: cluster create
commands.summary: Create a cluster and stamp its proxy app from the Caddy template.

flags.desc: Public hostname.
dns_rr | cloudflare | aws_alb.
Number of proxy replicas.

commands.name: cluster destroy
commands.summary: Destroy a cluster (proxy app stays unless --with-proxy).

flags.desc: Also destroy the proxy app.

commands.name: cluster member
commands.summary: Add, drain, undrain, remove, or reweight member apps.

examples.desc: Existing connections finish, no new ones.
Set the member's load-balancer weight. 0 = no traffic (true drain); used for blue/green flips.

commands.name: cluster deploy
commands.summary: Deploy across cluster members.

flags.desc: parallel (default) | rolling. Rolling drains then deploys one at a time.

commands.name: cluster autodeploy
commands.summary: Show or toggle cluster autodeploy. When enabled, a push to the cluster's repo fires a zero-downtime rolling release across members (drain, deploy, health-gate, restore) instead of deploying any single member in place. The Heroku "automatic deploys" analog, made safe for a self-hosted cluster.

flags.desc: Only auto-deploy pushes to this branch. Omit to accept whatever branch each member app is configured for; pass an empty value to clear the filter.

commands.notes:
Requires the GitHub webhook (autobuild) to be installed on at least
one member app so deliveries arrive. Autodeploy then routes that
delivery to a rolling cluster release rather than an in-place deploy.
A push is ignored while a previous rolling release is still in flight.

examples.desc: Show the current autodeploy state.
Enable rolling autodeploy on push.
Enable, restricted to the main branch.
Disable autodeploy.

commands.name: cluster status
commands.summary: Topology view of a cluster (variants, upstreams, live state).

commands.name: cluster domain
commands.summary: Manage the hostnames a cluster fronts. A cluster can serve many hostnames; cutover happens atomically across all of them.

commands.name: cluster variant
commands.summary: Manage named swappable backend groups (e.g. heroku, havenhelix). Each variant has weight (0 standby, >0 receives traffic) and status (active or draining).

flags.desc: Traffic weight, 0..1000. 0 = standby.
active | draining (draining stops new connections).
Human-readable note.

commands.name: cluster upstream
commands.summary: Manage per-variant, per-domain routes. An upstream is either an internal OwnStack app or an external HTTPS URL. Exactly one of --url or --app is required.

flags.desc: Variant this upstream belongs to.
Restrict to one hostname; omit to serve all hostnames on the cluster.
External upstream (https://...). Use for routing to a Heroku / external backend.
Internal OwnStack app (resolves to its stack IPs).
Upstream weight 0..1000.
Host header to send upstream (defaults to the cluster hostname).

commands.name: cluster switch
commands.summary: Atomically flip the active variant; reweights everything inside a transaction and fires one reconcile. All hostnames cutover together.

flags.desc: Name of the variant that should receive 100% of traffic.

examples.desc: Roll back in seconds if the new variant misbehaves.

groups.title: App management
groups.body: Operations on apps already in the control plane.

commands.name: app list
commands.summary: List apps in the active account.

commands.name: app stacks
commands.summary: List the stacks an app is deployed to.

commands.name: app test
commands.summary: Run the app's test command as a one-off process on its stack (Heroku `heroku run` style) and stream output live.

flags.desc: One-off command to run instead of the configured test_command.
Run on a specific stack (defaults to the app's primary/deployable stack).
Start the run and return immediately instead of streaming.
Print the created run as JSON (no streaming).

commands.notes:
Runs inside a fresh container booted from the app's deployed image,
so the test sees the real config vars and linked add-ons
(DATABASE_URL, REDIS_URL, ...). Exit code is captured via a sentinel
since `dokku run` does not forward it. Requires jq for live output.
Exit status: 0 passed, 1 failed, 2 timeout/unknown.

examples.desc: Run the configured test command and stream output.

examples.cmd: ownstack app test <APP> --command="bundle exec rspec spec/models"
examples.desc: One-off ad-hoc command.

commands.name: app test:list
commands.summary: List recent test runs (status, exit code, duration, command).

flags.desc: Raw JSON array.

commands.name: app test:show
commands.summary: Show a test run's details and full output.

flags.desc: Raw JSON.

commands.name: app test:config
commands.summary: Show or set the app's test command.

flags.desc: Set the test command (omit to just show current config).

commands.name: app destroy
commands.summary: Destroy a control-plane app and tear down its Dokku app + linked services on every stack it's deployed to.

flags.desc: Skip the interactive name-match confirmation.

commands.notes:
Server-side `AppDestroyWorker` runs the SSH cleanup
(apps:destroy plus postgres/mysql/redis/etc. unlink + destroy).
Persistent volumes are not removed automatically — host
`/var/lib/dokku/data/storage/*` dirs survive for now.

examples.desc: Interactive — prompts you to retype the name to confirm.
Non-interactive (CI/scripts).
Same as destroy, accepts UUID instead of name.

commands.name: app autodeploy
commands.summary: Show or toggle per-(app, stack) autodeploy. Turning autodeploy on also installs the GitHub push webhook if it isn't there yet — both layers have to be enabled for pushes to actually deploy.

flags.desc: Toggle on a single stack instead of every linked stack.

examples.desc: Show the per-stack autodeploy state.
Turn autodeploy on for every linked stack (and install the webhook if missing).
Turn it off for one stack.

commands.name: app autobuild
commands.summary: Manage the GitHub push webhook on an app's repository. Required for autodeploy to actually fire.
commands.notes:
GitHub autobuild and per-stack autodeploy are two independent layers.
Autobuild owns the webhook on the repo; autodeploy owns whether a
received webhook actually enqueues a deploy. `app autodeploy on` will
enable autobuild for you when both are off, but you can also manage
the webhook directly with these commands.

examples.desc: Show enabled state and the GitHub webhook id.
Install the GitHub push webhook on the app's repo.
Remove the webhook (deploys still work manually).

commands.name: app autobuild:consolidate
commands.summary: Collapse redundant per-app GitHub webhooks on the same repo down to a single survivor. Cleans up the per-app era for accounts that ran ahead of the share-on-create fix.
commands.notes:
Picks the lowest-id app's hook as canonical, deletes every other
GitHub-side hook for the same repo via the deploy-token user's
GitHub credentials, and re-points all sibling apps at the survivor.
--dry-run shows the plan without touching GitHub or the DB.

flags.desc: Report the plan but don't modify anything.

examples.desc: Consolidate the repo this app belongs to.
Preview the consolidation before doing it.

commands.name: app cron
commands.summary: Manage scheduled jobs that run via `dokku run <app> <command>` on the stack's crontab.
commands.notes:
The control plane keeps a managed block in the dokku user's crontab,
delimited by `# OWNSTACK-CRON: <dokku-app> BEGIN/END` markers. Any
add/update/remove triggers a re-push to every deployable stack. Use
`cron:sync` to force a re-push without changing the job set (useful
after restoring a stack or recovering from drift).

Schedule is standard 5-field cron (`minute hour day-of-month month
day-of-week`). Commands run inside `dokku run`, so use your app's
process binary directly (`rake db:cleanup`, `python manage.py jobs:run`).
`%` in the command must be escaped as `\%` — cron treats unescaped
`%` as a stdin separator.

examples.desc: List scheduled jobs.

examples.cmd: ownstack app cron:add <APP> '0 3 * * *' 'rake nightly' --description='nightly DB cleanup'
examples.desc: Add a nightly job at 03:00.
Health check every 15 minutes.
Change job
Pause a job without deleting it.
Resume a paused job.
Delete a job permanently.
Force re-push of the managed crontab block to every deployable stack.

commands.name: app nuke
commands.summary: Reset an app's Dokku-side state (apps:destroy + linked services) without touching the control-plane app, domains, env vars, or group.

flags.desc: Limit the nuke to a single stack instead of every stack the app deploys to.
Skip the interactive name-match confirmation.

commands.notes:
Use this when a Dokku build is stuck in a half-built state
(`Removing invalid image tag dokku/<app>:latest` with zero
buildpack output between cleanup and failure). Server-side
`AppNukeWorker` SSHs into each stack and runs `apps:destroy --force`
plus `postgres/redis/etc.:destroy` on linked services. The next
deploy rebuilds from scratch.

examples.desc: Interactive — prompts you to retype the name to confirm.
Non-interactive.
Only nuke state on one stack (use when an app is on multiple stacks).

commands.name: app clone
commands.summary: Clone an app — independent copy with its own database.

flags.desc: Target stack.
Custom domain TLD.
Trigger deploy after creation.

commands.name: app replicate
commands.summary: Replicate an app — shares its database and Redis.

commands.name: ssl-enable
commands.summary: Enable Let's Encrypt for the linked app.

flags.desc: Let's Encrypt account email.

commands.name: app group
commands.summary: Show or set the AppGroup an app belongs to.
commands.notes:
AppGroups are the sidebar groupings you see in the dashboard. Each app can belong
to one group (or none). The group is resolved by case-insensitive name or numeric id
within the current account. With no arguments, the app must be linked or passed
explicitly, and the current group is printed.

flags.desc: Remove the app from any group (sets app_group_id to null).

examples.desc: Show the linked app's current group.
Show the named app's current group.
Assign by case-insensitive group name.
Assign by numeric group id.
Remove from any group.

commands.name: app groups
commands.summary: List app groups in the active account (id, name, app count, parent).

groups.title: SSL certificates
groups.body:
Certificates live in an account-scoped "locker" (`/api/ssl_certificates`).
Each cert can be pushed to one or more apps; pushing writes the cert into
Dokku's per-app `tls/` directory and rebuilds nginx with a `listen 443 ssl`
block. Pushes are asynchronous — the CLI enqueues a `SslCertificatePushWorker`
job and polls for the result before reporting success/fail per stack.

commands.name: cert publish
commands.summary: Upload a sslgen cert (fullchain.pem + privkey.pem) to the locker, then push to an app.
commands.notes:
Each invocation uploads a new locker entry. To attach the same cert
to multiple apps without duplicates, run `cert publish` once and then
`cert push --cert-id=N --app=APP` for each additional app.

flags.desc: App name or id to push the cert to. Required.
Directory containing fullchain.pem and privkey.pem. Defaults to ~/.sslgen/certs/ownstack.org.
Display name for the locker entry. Defaults to basename of --cert-dir.
Push only to the given stack instead of every stack the app deploys to.

commands.name: cert list
commands.summary: List certificates in the locker (id, name, not-after).

commands.name: cert push
commands.summary: Push an existing locker certificate to an app — no re-upload, no duplicate locker entries.
commands.notes:
Use this when you already uploaded a wildcard cert (e.g., for
*.apps.example.com) and just want to attach it to a new app.

flags.desc: Locker certificate id (from `ownstack cert list`). Required.
App name or id. Required.
Push to one stack instead of every deployable stack on the app.

examples.desc: Find the cert id you want to reuse.
Attach an existing cert to another app.

commands.name: cert renew
commands.summary: Re-issue a locker certificate via Let's Encrypt and re-push it to every app using it.
commands.notes:
Requires a connected DNSimple integration (`ownstack dnsimple connect`).
The control plane answers the DNS-01 challenge with TXT records in your
DNSimple account, updates the locker entry in place, and re-pushes to all
apps whose `ssl_certificate_id` references the cert. Issuance takes a few
minutes; the CLI polls until the job completes. Certs expiring within 30
days renew automatically when auto-renew is on — this command is for
renewing on demand.

flags.desc: Locker certificate id (from `ownstack cert list`).
Instead of one cert, renew every locker cert expiring within the account's renewal window (the on-demand version of the daily sweep; works with auto-renew off).

examples.desc: Find the expiring cert id.
Re-issue and re-push in one shot.
Renew everything inside the renewal window now.

commands.name: dnsimple
commands.summary: Manage the account's DNSimple integration (powers Let's Encrypt issuance and auto-renewal).
commands.notes:
One DNSimple connection per account. The token must be a DNSimple
*account* token (DNSimple → Account → Automation → API tokens); user
tokens are rejected because they carry no account identity. With
auto-renew on (the default), a daily job re-issues any locker cert
expiring within 30 days whose domains are hosted in the connected
DNSimple account, then re-pushes it to the apps using it. Same
integration as Settings → Integrations → DNSimple in the dashboard.

flags.desc: For `connect` — the DNSimple account API token. Prompts on stdin if omitted (keeps the secret out of shell history).
For `auto-renew` — renew certs expiring within N days (1-89, default 30). Combinable with on|off.

examples.desc: Show connection and auto-renew state.
Link a DNSimple account token (prompted).
Pause automatic renewals; `on` re-enables.
Widen the renewal window to 45 days before expiry.
Remove the integration.

commands.name: route53
commands.summary: Manage the account's Route53 integration (powers Let's Encrypt issuance and auto-renewal).
commands.notes:
One Route53 connection per account, mirroring the DNSimple
integration. The IAM key needs route53:ListHostedZones,
route53:ListResourceRecordSets, and route53:ChangeResourceRecordSets.
Renewal picks whichever connected integration (DNSimple or Route53)
covers the cert's domains, so accounts can connect both. Same
integration as Settings → Integrations → Route53 in the dashboard.

flags.desc: For `connect` — reuse a saved AWS credential from the account's credential vault instead of pasting a key pair.
For `connect` — the AWS access key id (when not using --credential).
For `connect` — the AWS secret. Prompts on stdin if omitted (keeps the secret out of shell history).
For `connect` — AWS region for the client (Route53 is global; default us-east-1).
For `auto-renew` — renew certs expiring within N days (1-89, default 30). Combinable with on|off.

examples.desc: Show connection and auto-renew state.
Link using a saved AWS credential.
Link a raw key pair (secret prompted).
Pause automatic renewals; `on` re-enables.
Widen the renewal window to 45 days before expiry.
Remove the integration.

groups.title: Pipelines (staging → production)
groups.body:
Heroku-style staging → production flow. Each app gets one pipeline (created on first
deploy or via `add-staging`). Stages are tagged by name — anything matching /staging/i
is the staging stage; /prod(uction)?/i is production. The staging stage is a first-class
app (`<app>-staging`) with its own config vars, database, domains, and autobuild toggle:
leave autobuild on for staging and turn it off on production, and pushes to the tracked
branch deploy staging only. `promote` ships staging's exact image to production
(buildless, `dokku git:from-image`) when both stages share a stack; the production
release phase (migrations) runs against production config as usual.

commands.name: pipeline show
commands.summary: Print the app's pipeline stages with backing app, autobuild state, and last deploy.

examples.desc: Linked app.

commands.name: pipeline add-staging
commands.summary: Create a `<app>-staging` app on the production stack and deploy the current branch to it.

flags.desc: Staging-only custom domain (repeatable). Falls back to pilot domain if omitted.
Override a staging config var (repeatable). Empty value removes the key. Production config vars are copied minus service-link vars (DATABASE_URL etc.).

examples.desc: Add staging for the linked app.

commands.notes:
App must have a successful production deploy first. The production stage is
auto-created on first deploy; staging reuses the same stack. The staging app gets
its own postgres/redis links and rides the production repo webhook for autobuild.
To stop production from auto-deploying on push, run
`ownstack app autobuild:disable <app>` and keep autobuild on for `<app>-staging`.

commands.name: pipeline promote
commands.summary: Ship staging's release to production (buildless image promote when possible).
commands.notes:
Same-stack stages: the running staging image is pinned
(`dokku/<prod>:promote-<sha>`) and deployed via `dokku git:from-image`, so the
exact tested artifact ships with production config vars and release phase
(migrations). Different stacks or missing image: falls back to rebuilding
staging's last successful commit. Refuses while a staging deploy is in flight.
Output says which strategy ran. Tail with `ownstack remote deploy-log`.

commands.name: pipeline stage:create
commands.summary: Add a custom stage to the pipeline.

flags.desc: Stage label (required). Use "staging" or "production" to get role detection.
Dokku app name on the target stack (required unless --app is given).
Couple an existing account app as this stage (app-backed stage with its own settings).
Target stack name or id.
Sort order (0 = staging, 1 = production by convention).
Custom domain (repeatable).

examples.desc: Build a QA stage on a different stack.
Couple an existing app as the staging stage.

commands.name: pipeline stage:update
commands.summary: Patch a stage's name, dokku app, stack, position, or domains.

flags.desc: Stage id (required, from `pipeline show`).
Repeatable. Passing any --domain replaces the full list.

commands.name: pipeline stage:destroy
commands.summary: Delete a stage from the pipeline.

flags.desc: Stage id (required).

commands.notes:
Removing an app-backed stage does not destroy the backing app; destroy it
separately with `ownstack app destroy` if you no longer want it.

groups.title: Database (Postgres)
groups.body:
Manage the linked Postgres service on the stack: health checks, dumps,
lifecycle, and local tunnels. Postgres-only today; MySQL apps use
`db dump` but not the other verbs.

commands.name: db check
commands.summary: Check connectivity and auth on each stack the app is deployed to.

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).
Limit the check to one stack.

commands.name: db connections
commands.summary: Show Postgres connection usage vs max_connections, the active/idle/idle-in-txn breakdown, and top consumers by application_name.

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).
Emit raw numbers as JSON for scripting or dashboards.

commands.notes:
Connection saturation is a common cause of deploy failures on these
stacks: during a deploy the old containers keep running, so the
release one-off has to fit on top of the steady-state worker pools.
If usage sits near max_connections the release task dies with
"FATAL: sorry, too many clients already". This command makes that
visible without dropping to `dokku postgres:connect` + raw SQL.
Warns at >70% of max_connections, flags red at >85%.

commands.name: db config:show
commands.summary: Show OwnStack-managed Postgres settings and current live values.

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).
Emit { managed, live } as JSON.

commands.notes:
Goes through the control plane (which SSHes to the stack), so no local SSH access is
needed. The CONTEXT column shows whether a parameter needs a restart (postmaster) or
just a reload. MANAGED values are the ones OwnStack re-applies if the DB is recreated.

commands.name: db config:set
commands.summary: Set Postgres settings; persisted as managed (survive a recreate) and applied live.

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).
Apply now but defer any restart-required change to the next restart.
Emit the result as JSON.

examples.cmd: ownstack db config:set myapp shared_buffers=12GB effective_cache_size=36GB

commands.notes:
Each setting is stored on the app and re-applied on top of the sized provisioning
defaults whenever the Postgres service is (re)created, so a postgres:destroy/recreate no
longer reverts your tuning to compiled defaults. The control plane applies the change via
ALTER SYSTEM and then reloads, or restarts only when a changed parameter requires it
(e.g. shared_buffers, shared_preload_libraries). Use --no-restart to stage a
restart-required change without bouncing Postgres now.

commands.name: db repair
commands.summary: Re-link DB, resync the postgres role password if drifted, restart the app.

flags.desc: App name.
Target stack.

commands.name: db dump
commands.summary: Stream a Postgres or MySQL dump to a local file via the control plane.

flags.desc: App name.
Target stack.
Local file (default = Content-Disposition filename from server).

commands.notes: Postgres dumps are pg_dump custom format (.dump); MySQL dumps are SQL (.sql).

commands.name: db tunnel
commands.summary: Forward a local TCP port to the app's Dokku-internal Postgres on the stack.

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).
Target stack (required when the app spans multiple stacks).
Local port to bind. Defaults to 15432.

examples.desc: Tunnel postgres-myapp to localhost:15432.
Pick a custom local port.

commands.notes:
Uses the same SSH transport as `ssh stack`. Reads the Dokku DSN to
construct a localhost-keyed connection string, then runs `ssh -L`
in the foreground. Ctrl-C closes the tunnel. Equivalent to
`heroku pg:psql --tunnel` for Heroku users.

commands.name: db version
commands.summary: Show the Postgres image version for the linked service.

commands.name: db status
commands.summary: Show service status (alias of `db version`).

commands.name: db logs
commands.summary: Tail the Postgres container logs.

commands.name: db create
commands.summary: Create the postgres-<app> service on the linked stack.

flags.desc: Postgres major version (default 17).

commands.name: db destroy
commands.summary: Unlink and destroy the postgres service. Confirms by service name.

flags.desc: Skip the interactive confirmation.

commands.name: db recreate
commands.summary: Destroy + create + link (all data lost). Requires interactive confirmation.

flags.desc: Postgres major version of the new service.

commands.name: db link
commands.summary: Link the postgres-<app> service to the app and set DATABASE_URL.

commands.name: db backups
commands.summary: List Heroku Postgres backups (Heroku app, not the ownstack one).

flags.desc: Heroku app to list backups from.
ownstack app context (for auth/permissions).

commands.name: db backups:capture
commands.summary: Trigger a fresh Heroku backup.

commands.name: db restore
commands.summary: Restore from a Heroku backup or an arbitrary dump URL.

flags.desc: Specific Heroku backup number (default = latest).
Dump URL (alternative to --heroku-app).

commands.name: db restore:info
commands.summary: Show progress and logs for a restore job. --follow streams live.

commands.name: db restore:cancel
commands.summary: Cancel an in-progress restore.

commands.name: db restores
commands.summary: List recent restore jobs.

groups.title: Postgres backups (object storage)
groups.body:
Per-app Postgres backups to object storage (S3, GCS, or DreamObjects),
mirroring Heroku's `pg:backups`: capture, list, download, restore, and a
scheduled backup with a grandfather-father-son (GFS) retention calendar.

Backups are captured on the stack via `dokku postgres:export`, uploaded to
the object storage configured for the app's stack, and pruned on the
retention calendar. Each backup has a short id (e.g. `b042`) and is
individually downloadable and restorable. The destination credential and
bucket come from the stack's backup configuration unless overridden.

commands.name: pg backups
commands.summary: List this app's Postgres backups (id, date, size, status, type).

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).

commands.name: pg backups:capture
commands.summary: Capture a new backup now and upload it to object storage.

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).
Capture from a specific stack (default = first deployable stack).

commands.name: pg backups:info
commands.summary: Show one backup's details (status, phase, uploaded/size, service, timestamps).

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).
Live-update a one-line status (phase + bytes uploaded) until the backup completes or fails.

commands.name: pg backups:download
commands.summary: Download a backup dump to a local file via a presigned URL.

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).
Local file to write (default = the backup's filename).

commands.name: pg backups:url
commands.summary: Print a time-limited presigned download URL for a backup.

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).

commands.name: pg backups:restore
commands.summary: Restore a stored backup into the app's database (overwrites current data).

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).
Restore into a specific stack (default = the backup's stack).

commands.notes:
Reuses the same restore pipeline as `db restore --url=...`: the control
plane signs a download URL for the backup and streams it into the
target Postgres. Track progress with `ownstack db restore:info ID --follow`.

commands.name: pg backups:delete
commands.summary: Delete a backup and its object-storage dump.

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).

commands.name: pg backups:schedule
commands.summary: Enable scheduled backups with a GFS retention calendar.

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).
Daily UTC time (e.g. 02:00). Converted to a cron expression.
Full cron expression (UTC), overrides --at.
Keep the newest backup of each of the last N days (default 7).
Keep the newest backup of each of the last N ISO weeks (default 4).
Keep the newest backup of each of the last N months (default 6).
Keep the newest backup of each of the last N years (default 0).
Send backups to a managed bucket (owned or shared) instead of the stack default. See `ownstack bucket`.

commands.notes:
A backup is retained if it is the newest in any kept calendar bucket,
so a single backup can satisfy the daily, weekly, monthly and yearly
slots at once. Everything outside the kept buckets is pruned from both
object storage and the backup list.

commands.name: pg backups:schedules
commands.summary: Show the current schedule and retention calendar.

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).

commands.name: pg backups:unschedule
commands.summary: Disable scheduled backups (existing backups are kept).

flags.desc: App name (defaults to the linked app).

groups.title: Object-storage buckets
groups.body:
First-class object-storage buckets (S3, GCS, DreamObjects) owned by your
account and shareable to other accounts, just like stacks. A share recipient
gets their own isolated prefix inside the bucket: they can only ever see and
write their own objects, never anyone else's; the owner sees everything.

Buckets are the recommended destination for per-app database backups
(`ownstack pg backups`). OwnStack never lends its own storage implicitly: if
another account wants backups, they bring (or are shared) a bucket.

commands.name: bucket list
commands.summary: List buckets you own or have accepted a share to.

commands.name: bucket create
commands.summary: Register a bucket backed by one of your object-storage credentials.

flags.desc: Object-storage credential (S3/GCS/DreamObjects) in your account.
Bucket region (e.g. us-west-2).
Also create the underlying bucket in object storage (not just the record).
Friendly display name.

commands.name: bucket rm
commands.summary: Remove the bucket record. The underlying object-storage bucket is left intact.

commands.name: bucket share
commands.summary: Invite another account by email. They get an isolated prefix on accept.

commands.name: bucket shares
commands.summary: List a bucket's shares with status (pending / accepted) and prefix.

commands.name: bucket unshare
commands.summary: Revoke a share. The account loses access (its objects remain under its prefix).

groups.title: SSH access
groups.body: Interactive SSH via the control plane — no local SSH key needed. Credentials come from the API.

commands.name: ssh app
commands.summary: Shell into an app container (dokku enter), or run a one-off command with --run.

flags.desc: Target stack (multi-stack apps).
Enter a specific process (default web).

Run COMMAND in the running container instead of opening a shell. The command is streamed over stdin (so quotes/parens/semicolons are never mangled) and run with the app's buildpack env, so rails/rake/bundle resolve.

examples.cmd: ownstack ssh app my-app --run 'rails runner "puts User.count"'

commands.notes:
--run requires host (sudo docker) access on the stack, which the standard
ubuntu stack SSH user has; it is unavailable on stacks whose app SSH user is
the restricted dokku ForceCommand user.

commands.name: ssh stack
commands.summary: SSH into a stack host as the ubuntu user.

groups.title: LLM addons
groups.body:
Self-hosted language models running on stacks you own. Apps link to them
via env vars (LLM_URL, LLM_MODEL, LLM_API_KEY). The endpoint speaks the
OpenAI-compatible API on top of llama.cpp server.

Topology: `dedicated` (addon owns its stack, stop = instance off) or
`colocated` (addon shares a stack with apps, stop = process only).

commands.name: llm list
commands.summary: List LLM addons on this account with model, status, topology, and cost state.

commands.name: llm create
commands.summary: Create an LLM addon. Provisioning runs in the background.

flags.desc: Curated model id (default qwen-2.5-7b). Or `hf:org/repo[/file.gguf]` for HuggingFace.
Default dedicated (own stack, stop = instance off).
Required for colocated; the existing stack to share.
Cloud provider when creating a dedicated stack.

examples.desc: Default model on a new dedicated stack.
Smaller model for tighter RAM budgets.
Colocate on stack 42 (inference competes with apps).
Arbitrary HuggingFace GGUF (no guarantees).

commands.name: llm link
commands.summary: Inject LLM_URL/LLM_MODEL/LLM_API_KEY into an app.

flags.desc: App to link the addon to.

commands.name: llm unlink
commands.summary: Remove the LLM_* env vars from an app.

flags.desc: App to unlink.

commands.name: llm start
commands.summary: Start the inference container. Also serves as `wake` for stopped addons.

commands.name: llm update
commands.summary: Update addon settings (auto-wake / idle timeout) without restarting.

flags.desc: Disable auto-wake.
Re-enable auto-wake.
Set idle-stop timeout in seconds.

commands.name: llm stop
commands.summary: Stop the inference container.

flags.desc: Disable auto-wake; the addon won't restart on incoming requests.
(dedicated) keep the host instance running; only stop the process.

commands.name: llm logs
commands.summary: Print provisioning and runtime logs for an addon.

flags.desc: Poll the logs endpoint every 3 seconds and stream new content.

commands.name: llm info
commands.summary: Show full state including cost, model metadata, endpoint URL.

commands.name: llm destroy
commands.summary: Tear down the addon. Dedicated stacks also torn down unless --keep-stack.

flags.desc: (dedicated) keep the underlying stack.

commands.name: llm models
commands.summary: Show the curated model list (size, RAM, license).

commands.name: llm diagnose
commands.summary: Ask the linked LLM to diagnose a failed deployment from its log tail.

flags.desc: Deployment id to diagnose.
If the addon is waking, poll up to ~50s for the result rather than returning a pending response.

groups.title: Voice addons
groups.body:
Self-hosted Moonshine speech-to-text running on stacks you own. Apps link
to them via env vars (SPEECH_URL, SPEECH_MODEL, SPEECH_API_KEY) and call an
OpenAI-compatible endpoint (POST {SPEECH_URL}/audio/transcriptions). CPU-only
(no GPU), so colocated is cheap. Pairs with the LLM addon for voice agents:
speech to text, then the LLM, then text to speech.

Topology: `dedicated` (addon owns its stack, stop = instance off) or
`colocated` (addon shares a stack with apps, stop = process only).

commands.name: voice list
commands.summary: List voice addons on this account with model, status, topology, and cost state.

commands.name: voice create
commands.summary: Create a voice addon. Provisioning builds the image on the stack in the background.

flags.desc: Curated Moonshine language pack (default moonshine-en).
Default dedicated (own stack, stop = instance off).
Required for colocated; the existing stack to share.
Cloud provider when creating a dedicated stack.

examples.desc: English speech-to-text on a new dedicated stack.
Spanish language pack.
Colocate on stack 42 (cheap, CPU-only).

commands.name: voice link
commands.summary: Inject SPEECH_URL/SPEECH_MODEL/SPEECH_API_KEY into an app.

flags.desc: App to link the addon to.

commands.name: voice unlink
commands.summary: Remove the SPEECH_* env vars from an app.

flags.desc: App to unlink.

commands.name: voice start
commands.summary: Start the speech container. Also serves as `wake` for stopped addons.

commands.name: voice update
commands.summary: Update addon settings (auto-wake / idle timeout) without restarting.

flags.desc: Disable auto-wake.
Re-enable auto-wake.
Set idle-stop timeout in seconds.

commands.name: voice stop
commands.summary: Stop the speech container.

flags.desc: Disable auto-wake; the addon won't restart on incoming requests.
(dedicated) keep the host instance running; only stop the process.

commands.name: voice logs
commands.summary: Print provisioning (image build) and runtime logs for an addon.

flags.desc: Poll the logs endpoint every 3 seconds and stream new content.

commands.name: voice info
commands.summary: Show full state including language, cost, model metadata, endpoint URL.

commands.name: voice destroy
commands.summary: Tear down the addon. Dedicated stacks also torn down unless --keep-stack.

flags.desc: (dedicated) keep the underlying stack.

commands.name: voice models
commands.summary: Show the curated Moonshine model list (language, RAM, license).

groups.title: Open Source templates
groups.body:
One-click installs for popular open-source apps. Each template declares its
official Docker image and required services (Postgres, Redis, etc.). Running
`ownstack oss install` creates a new OwnStack app on a provisioned stack,
links the required services, and deploys the image. Same catalog the
Extras → Open Source page in the web UI consumes.

What install does for you:
  - Creates the OwnStack app with the right needs_* flags so deploys
    install / link the right Dokku plugin services automatically.
  - Generates any required secrets (placeholders like SECRET_KEY,
    MEILI_MASTER_KEY) with SecureRandom.hex.
  - Mounts persistent volumes declared by the template (Ollama models,
    Qdrant storage, etc.) via `dokku storage:mount` before the deploy.
  - For templates that need a post-link env-var copy (currently Metabase:
    MB_DB_CONNECTION_URI from DATABASE_URL), a worker fires ~45 s after
    the initial deploy lands, sets the derived vars, and rebuilds.

commands.name: oss list
commands.summary: List one-click open-source templates.

commands.name: oss show
commands.summary: Show image, required services, env vars, persistent volumes, and docs URL for a single template.

commands.name: oss install
commands.summary: Install a template onto an existing provisioned stack.
commands.notes:
The picked stack must be provisioned. If you don't have one yet, open
/stacks/new in the web UI or run `ownstack stack ...` to create one.
Required secrets are auto-generated; you can read them after install
with `dokku config:show <app>` over SSH or via the app's config in
the web UI.

flags.desc: New app name (lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens; start with a letter).
Target stack id. Must be provisioned.
Optional custom domain; defaults to the stack's default app TLD.

examples.desc: BI dashboards. Postgres auto-linked; MB_DB_CONNECTION_URI copied from DATABASE_URL ~45s after first deploy.
Local LLM runtime. /root/.ollama mounted from a persistent host volume.
Vector DB. /qdrant/storage mounted from a persistent host volume.

groups.title: AI assistant context
groups.body: Generate context files (CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules) so AI assistants understand the app layout.

commands.name: ai:setup
commands.summary: Generate AI assistant context files for the current app.

examples.desc: Generate both CLAUDE.md and .cursorrules (default).
Just CLAUDE.md.
Just .cursorrules.

commands.notes: Run from inside an OwnStack app directory (must have .ownstack-config).

groups.title: Authentication
groups.body:
Sign in to the control plane. Tokens are stored in ~/.ownstack/config, or per
account under ~/.ownstack/profiles/<name> when using profiles.

commands.name: login
commands.summary: Authenticate with the control plane.

flags.desc: Direct token from Settings → API token.
With --token, save the token as profile NAME and switch the active profile to it. To add a profile WITHOUT switching, use `ownstack profile add`.

examples.desc: Browser device-code flow.

commands.name: profile
commands.summary: Manage multiple control-plane accounts (list, show, use, add).
commands.body:
Profiles let one machine hold tokens for several control-plane accounts.
Files live in ~/.ownstack/profiles/<name>. The active profile is recorded
in ~/.ownstack/config; any command can override it for a single invocation
with the OWNSTACK_PROFILE environment variable, which is the safest pattern
when multiple terminals or agents share the machine.

`profile add` creates a profile WITHOUT changing the active profile, so
concurrent work against other accounts is never disturbed. By contrast
`ownstack login --token TOKEN --profile NAME` creates the profile AND
switches the active profile to it.

flags.desc: (add) API token for the new profile. Prompted for interactively if omitted.
(add) Control-plane URL. Defaults to the current config's URL.
(add) Overwrite an existing profile of the same name.

examples.desc: List profiles, active one marked.
Add a profile without switching to it.
Switch the active profile.
One-off command against a profile without switching.

groups.title: Discovery and self-description
groups.body: Commands that describe the CLI itself, for humans and agents.

commands.name: whatami
commands.summary: Print an AI-friendly markdown brief of the CLI (built from this manifest).

examples.desc: Print full brief.
Just the overview.

commands.name: docs
commands.summary: Export the docs manifest as JSON or YAML.

flags.desc: JSON output (default).
YAML output (the source file as-is).

examples.desc: Pipe into a docs site build step.

commands.name: help
commands.summary: Show top-level help.

workflows.title: First-time setup
steps: Install the CLI (curl install or local clone).
Run `ownstack login` and complete the browser flow.
Run `ownstack apps` to confirm you can see your account.

workflows.title: Generate and deploy a new app from scratch
steps: `cd myapp && ownstack git push --name=myapp --app=myapp` — push to ownstack Git and create the app.
`ownstack deploy --wait` — kick off the first deploy and stream the log.

workflows.title: Adopt an existing local repo into the control plane
steps: `ownstack remote -a my-app` — link the repo to an existing app.

workflows.title: Promote staging to production
steps: `ownstack pipeline add-staging` — creates the staging stage on the same stack and kicks off a staging deploy.
Test the staging deploy at its assigned domain (or pilot URL).
`ownstack pipeline promote` — re-deploys staging's last successful commit to the production stage.
`ownstack remote deploy-log` to tail the build.

workflows.title: Debug a failing deploy
steps: `ownstack remote deploy-log` — see the build/release log.
If the build failed, fix locally, commit, and `ownstack deploy --wait` again.
If the release (e.g. migrations) failed, fix in code and redeploy; for db connectivity see `ownstack ssh app <name>` or the Data → Health tab in the UI.

linked_marker.desc: Contains the linked app id. Created by `ownstack remote -a`.

gitignore.desc: Add `.ownstack-config` to .gitignore if it contains tokens.
